IMPORTANT COPY PREMISES
I want to go into the basic premises for writing winning copy.
I don't think writing winning sales copy is hard.
And I don't think it takes a long time.
Instead, I think it's all about our process.
I think most of us over complicate things dramatically and, in fact, I don't just think that, I know that. I've
worked with tons of copywriters who will take all these elements and put all this pressure on themselves to be
like a creative genius at every step of the way, when that's the last thing that’s needed.
Creativity is amazing and encouraged with copy, and it's fun to be creative, but you don't need to be a genius
to write really strong winning sales copy.
Perfection is also the enemy, and that doesn't just go for copy. In my opinion, that goes for business, that goes
for life; all that kind of stuff.
When you're writing copy, trying to be perfect, taking two months for a promo that was good in two weeks, is
just an absolute waste of time.

A classic example of this, in the energy space, is the whole Tesla energy generator which some of you may have
seen.
Big energy didn't want his solutions, so he was discredited and shamed, and his inventions and discoveries were
thrown into the ash heap of history, until now.
Now we've got, oh yay! We’ve found Tesla's energy generator and now you can get free energy.
Another example is the foot fungus offer that I’ve done recently.
See, the US Army in WWII was trying to cure foot fungus; this is true, there was a desperate race to try and
cure foot fungus.
In fact, 8% of the armed forces, at any given time, were out of service because they had athlete's foot,
ringworm, and jock itch, so they kept going to the medic.
This is in the height of WWII, where literally the fate of civilization was on the line, so this was a huge
problem.
So the sergeant general, part of the army, was on this desperate race to try to find a cure for foot fungus. As silly
as it sounds, it's completely true. There's a crazy number of archives and medical army history that talks about
this. Now that's amazing copy. So here I am with this foot fungus offer, and the product is FDA approved and
I'm like, holy shit, that's awesome.
I love looking back to history. Try to find an old quest someone else has taken to discover a solution to
what you’re solving.
Maybe that old solution was forgotten or hidden.
That's the kind of stuff that's going to be really interesting.
So let your own curiosity flourish and help you out here.

You can take something from way back in the day, that was an awesome solution, and then take it back to
today, and maybe tweak it or improve it. Or you can help people to rediscover it, that's a really powerful copy
element.
Now, we’ll look at corruption to spark curiosity in your audience.
Ask yourself, is there a belief that the markets' pain point used to not exist or used to not be so bad?
But is this problem now worse or recently exasperated by outside forces? And what were those forces and
what's the reason behind their presence?
We see this in health all the time. Diabetes and obesity used to be uncommon, but now they're common. People
blame big corporations; they blame processed foods, they blame lazy Americans, they blame sugar. They
believe that the interests of big food corporations have tricked them into living an unhealthy lifestyle. That's a
belief that a lot of people have. There's corruption there; the Garden of Eden thing, right? We've fallen from the
Garden of Eden.
That’s classic Daniel Toh, they isolate a group of people that don’t struggle from whatever condition or
pain point that most of us in America do. Why don’t those people have the same problem?
We suffer, but the reason why is because we've been exposed to these outside forces; we've been corrupted.
Whereas there's this tribe in the Amazon, for example, that doesn't suffer. They have the largest penises in the
world because they didn't eat too much processed food, or whatever the reason is.
Or the renegade person who doesn't suffer from the same pain point as the rest of us, and he's managed to stay
free from a life of corruption while the rest of us haven't.
So even if that uncorrupted person is someone here in the US, for example, there's this doctor that's 95 and
doing 1000 pushups a day because he doesn't do this one thing that the rest of us do.
I'm looking for all that kind of stuff in the research; this curiosity, this corruption, these interesting differences
between groups or individuals, because it's all stuff that gets your wheels turning when you're going to write the
copy.

So, where do you find answers to research questions?
One place I love is forums and Reddit.
And I believe it’s more important to look at the # of replies something has, over the # of views. If a post has a
million views, that doesn't necessarily matter.
I want to know if people reply to it and if they were engaged. If they're like, I had that problem too or, my mom
had that problem and someone else jumps in and is like, here's how I solved it then other people jump in and
say, No you solved it wrong! If there's a debate going on, that's awesome.
I like to see replies where people are passionate, engaged, and interested.
Here’s an example from Reddit called fat people stories.
This is one of my favorite places on Reddit.
When I was work on any blood sugar, health, diet, or weight loss offer, I come here.
Here’s a fantastic thing I found that I was able to use in my copy:
I'm a nurse who primarily works within the environment of the operating room so I very rarely care for obese
patients these days. But the one thing that may be more common than MRSA in the hospital is the diabetic
patient. When I was working in the nursing floor, I saw Type 2 diabetic patients left and right and some never
cease to leave me in complete and utter awe at their propensity for killing themselves
…
Despite this, I cannot tell you how many Type 2 diabetic patients I have watched willingly kill themselves
because they don't want to make the lifestyle changes necessary to manage their condition. Not all Type 2
diabetics are the same and not all cases of diabetes are the same, but this is a reoccurring trend I see time and
again. One of my very first patients was an obese man who came into the hospital with a blood glucose reading
in the mid-400s; for reference, a non-fasting level should be less than 125. Surprisingly he was not admitted for
this, but instead for the cellulitis that developed on his leg that was infected with MRSA more commonly

referred to as Staph. Diabetes very commonly results in loss of feeling in the extremities, particularly the feet,

When I asked why not, he explained that it was a hassle and that he wanted to live his life and enjoy all the

give them up entirely. He winked at me and said, that isn't living. We treated him with antibiotics and sent him

skin infection that was so bad that it spread to the bone. You have to take your insulin, I told him. I talked to

And then she keeps going.

Alright here's some important notes on this research component:
Through your research, you're going to typically find a kernel for the mechanism behind the problem
and the solution and we're going to get into the problem and solution in a minute.
If you find a new solution to the problem and your product fits it, don't be afraid to tie it together. As an
example, if we think about metabolism, and everyone wants to speed up their metabolism, you find a lot of
research about how metabolism is governed by the thyroid hormone. You may want to bridge the gap and have
your copy talk about how your product helps the thyroid which is going to, therefore, regulate your metabolism.
So: How a dysfunctional thyroid is the real cause of your poor metabolism. Now you’re offering your readers
something they might not know, something that sparks their curiosity and that they want to learn more about.
Another note: rabbit holes are your friend, within reason.
That's actually one of my favorite parts about writing copy. In this stage that we’re talking about, I encourage
you to go down rabbit holes, within reason; and by within reason I mean you shouldn't start with this and by
an hour later you're looking at like who invented underwear or where the stars came from or whatever. That's
not a good rabbit hole, but is good as far as curiosity and interesting stuff.
I have an example here: Maybe you're looking at a DIY solar generator and you see someone’s name as an early
inventor of a prototype. I'd be like, ‘What was the story behind that?’ You look at the prototype and you found
out it had a novel mechanism of action. That's all great fodder for copy.
So, it's okay to drift along and jump from different places, and I think even looking at authority sites is great,
but I also think looking at really random small blogs, and small little websites and things like that, is fine
because you can find hidden gems there.

M: MECHANISM
Now you need the Mechanism.
A unique mechanism explains why our prospect is struggling to achieve a goal, and how they can overcome that
problem they're having.
It's a two-part logical connection.
1. 	And so it's really the mechanism behind the problem, that the avatar's facing, or your market is facing,
and…
2. 	There's a mechanism behind the solution.
Having just a mechanism behind the product is not enough, in my opinion.
Instead, show me, okay here's the problem, and you're overweight; well here's the real reason why and here's
the unique mechanism behind why you're overweight and once you have that, you're making a logical
connection to the solution. Now your solution is the best one because it deals with that real unique reason why
the avatar has this problem.
By the time our prospect comes to us, they've tried numerous solutions to their problem already. Those
solutions may not have worked well, or worked at all, or worked partially, but they worked for a period of time
before they stopped working so our job as a copywriter is to educate our prospect about the real reason why
past solutions didn't reap them permanent results.
One way we do this:
By explaining their knowledge about the problem is incomplete and they've been missing one crucial piece
of information and that this knowledge gap is what's been holding them back.
By showing the unique mechanism behind the problem, you get the prospects to understand why they've failed
in the past and by showing the unique mechanism behind the solution, you get the prospect to believe that our
product will bring success for other alternatives that fail.

Here’s my outline of a sales letter.
Now that I've done my research, I have my mechanism of the problem. I have my mechanism in the solution.
I've got my brief. Now, it's actually time to write the sales letter.
Right, you've got your lead, which most people have probably heard of.
You've got your background story.
You've got the unique mechanism of the problem.
You've got the unique mechanism of the solution. You've got the product build-up and reveal.
You've got the close and you've got FAQs.
So I'm going to go through these and explain stuff a bit. And then we're going to look at several sales letter
examples that will show you all these. And I will say that this is not set in stone. This is a good checklist, if
you want to write good copy repeatedly, then go through and use this as a checklist, it's going to be
awesome. But you'll see that through these examples, sometimes things are in a little different order.
Sometimes, I throw some things in sometimes I leave things out. But you'll also notice that no matter
what, usually almost all of these elements are going to be in a sales copy.
And one other thing – the order of these elements can change. You don't have to call out the problem

you put a period. It sounds really silly, but a lot of copywriters, even good ones, I found, have trouble with
ellipses. Where they keep putting ellipses, keep putting ellipses. So it kind of keeps continuing, but then all of a

or the idea behind all of that. So, as simple as that sounds. And also, if you read it out loud, and you think about
every time you're switching from one idea or thought to the next one, there should be a period there. I know it
sounds simple, but I swear to God, it increases conversions by having something that flows well.
